evidence_quotes.quote: A recent example that really stuck with me was the shutdown of The Crew (2014) by Ubisoft.
we buy games assuming we own them

paragraphs.text: Dear European Commission, As a lifelong gamer and European citizen, Im writing to urge you to include specific protections against the premature shutdown of digital games in the upcoming Digital Fairness Act (DFA). I believe this is an overlooked but very real issue that affects millions of people who buy, play, and invest in online games across Europe. Ive personally experienced what it feels like to lose access to a game I paid for and enjoyed, simply because the publisher decided to shut it down. Its frustrating, disappointing, and honestly feels unfairespecially when theres no option for a refund, no offline version, and no way to keep playing, even alone. A recent example that really stuck with me was the shutdown of The Crew (2014) by Ubisoft. Many players, myself included, had purchased the gamesome even just months before the announcement. But in April 2024, the servers went offline and the entire game became inaccessible, even for solo play. No compensation, no option to download a legacy version, nothing. Years of progress and enjoyment disappeared overnight. This kind of thing is happening more and more, and it leaves players powerless. Were paying for games, building communities, and spending countless hoursonly for everything to be wiped out without warning or recourse. It creates a deep mistrust between users and publishers. Why this matters to me (and to others like me): Were misled: Many of us buy games assuming we own them, but it turns out were just renting accesswith no guarantee. It wastes our time and money: Some players spend hundreds of euros on in-game purchases and progress. We lose more than a game: These are communities, social spaces, and creative outlets. Its not just softwareits part of our lives. What I hope the DFA will include: Rules about shutdowns: Require companies to give advance notice, offer refunds or offline modes, and explain clearly why a game is being removed. Preservation options: Let users or communities keep the game alive, especially if official support ends. A game doesnt need to die just because a publisher moves on. Better transparency: If a game we pay for is essentially temporary, that needs to be clearly stated before purchase. Fair access rights: If we pay for something, we should be able to use iteven after official servers go offline. I love the idea behind the Digital Fairness Act. But digital fairness should include this too. Games are not just productstheyre experiences, friendships, memories. And right now, companies can take those away from us, permanently, without consequence. That doesnt feel fair. Thank you for considering this perspective. I hope youll help protect not just our digital rights, but also the things we care about in the digital spaces we live in. Warm regards, Marek
